quiz.title: High-School Geometry: Unit 7 Quiz 3
quiz.subtitle: Using Two Pairs of Congruent Angles to Show Similarity
quiz.subject: Geometry
quiz.topic: Geometry: 7.3 Proving Triangles Similar: AA Postulate

questions.question: The AA Similarity Postulate states that two triangles are similar if:
options: Two sides of one triangle are proportional to two sides of another
Two angles of one triangle are congruent to two angles of another
All three sides of one triangle are congruent to all sides of another
One angle and one side of one triangle match those of another triangle
questions.explanation: AA (Angle-Angle) states that if two angles of one triangle are congruent to two angles of another triangle, the triangles are similar. The third angles are automatically congruent by the Triangle Angle Sum Theorem.

questions.question: Why does the AA Postulate only require two pairs of congruent angles?
options: The third angles are irrelevant since similarity depends only on two pairs
The third angles are assumed to be supplementary rather than congruent
The third angles must match because all triangle angle sums equal 180^\circ
The third angles can vary without affecting the proportional side lengths
questions.explanation: If two angles of one triangle match two angles of another, the third angles are forced to match because both triangles' angles must sum to 180^\circ. So AA is sufficient.

questions.question: In $\triangle ABC$ and $\triangle DEF$, $\angle A \cong \angle D$ and $\angle B \cong \angle E$. What can be concluded?
options: The triangles are similar by the AA Similarity Postulate
The triangles are congruent by the ASA Congruence Postulate
The triangles are unrelated due to missing side length data
The triangles are similar by the SSS Similarity Theorem
questions.explanation: Two pairs of congruent angles satisfy the AA Similarity Postulate. Therefore, $\triangle ABC \sim \triangle DEF$.

questions.question: In $\triangle PQR$ and $\triangle STU$, $m\angle P = 45^\circ$, $m\angle Q = 80^\circ$, $m\angle S = 45^\circ$, and $m\angle T = 80^\circ$. Are the triangles similar?
options: No, because the proportional side lengths remain unverified
Yes, because all right triangles share identical angle measures
No, because only angle measures were provided for verification
Yes, because two pairs of corresponding angles are congruent
questions.explanation: $\angle P \cong \angle S = 45^\circ$ and $\angle Q \cong \angle T = 80^\circ$. Two pairs of congruent angles satisfy AA, so the triangles are similar.

questions.question: In $\triangle XYZ$, a line through points $A$ on $\overline{XZ}$ and $B$ on $\overline{YZ}$ is parallel to $\overline{XY}$. Which angle pairs are congruent?
options: $\angle Z \cong \angle A$ and $\angle Z \cong \angle B$ by vertical angle relationships
$\angle X \cong \angle ZAB$ and $\angle Y \cong \angle ZBA$ by corresponding angles
$\angle X \cong \angle Y$ and $\angle ZAB \cong \angle ZBA$ by alternate interior angles
No angles are congruent without additional parallel line data
questions.explanation: When a line is parallel to one side of a triangle and intersects the other two sides, corresponding angles are formed. $\angle X$ corresponds to $\angle ZAB$, and $\angle Y$ corresponds to $\angle ZBA$.

questions.question: In $\triangle ABC$, $m\angle A = 50^\circ$ and $m\angle B = 60^\circ$. In $\triangle DEF$, $m\angle D = 50^\circ$ and $m\angle E = 70^\circ$. Are the triangles similar?
options: No, because only one pair of corresponding angles is congruent
No, because the missing third angles prevent similarity testing
Yes, because one matching angle guarantees proportional sides
Yes, because both triangles share the same largest angle measure
questions.explanation: Only $\angle A \cong \angle D = 50^\circ$. The second pair does not match: $60^\circ \neq 70^\circ$. AA requires two pairs of congruent angles, so similarity cannot be established.

questions.question: Two right triangles each have an acute angle of $35^\circ$. Are they similar?
options: No, because right triangles require proportional hypotenuse lengths
No, because the remaining acute angles differ between the triangles
Yes, because both triangles share a 90^\circ angle and a 35^\circ angle
Yes, because all right triangles automatically satisfy AA similarity
questions.explanation: Each triangle has a 90^\circ angle and a 35^\circ angle. That's two pairs of congruent angles, satisfying AA. The triangles are similar regardless of side lengths.

questions.question: In $\triangle JKL$, $m\angle J = 40^\circ$ and $m\angle K = 85^\circ$. What must be true of any triangle similar to $\triangle JKL$?
options: It must contain side lengths that match the original triangle
It can contain any angle measures as long as one side matches
It must contain angle measures of 40^\circ and 85^\circ only
It must contain angle measures of 40^\circ, 85^\circ, and 55^\circ
questions.explanation: First find the third angle of $\triangle JKL$: 180^\circ - 40^\circ - 85^\circ = 55^\circ. Any similar triangle must have the same three angle measures: 40^\circ, 85^\circ, and 55^\circ.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a valid method to prove two triangles are similar?
options: The Angle-Angle Similarity Postulate
The Angle-Side-Angle Congruence Postulate
The Side-Angle-Side Similarity Theorem
The Side-Side-Side Similarity Theorem
questions.explanation: AA, SAS Similarity, and SSS Similarity are the three valid methods to prove triangle similarity. ASA is a congruence postulate, not a similarity theorem.

questions.question: Vertical angles are formed by intersecting lines, and two triangles share these vertical angles. What additional information proves the triangles are similar?
options: One additional pair of congruent angles in corresponding positions
One additional pair of proportional sides in corresponding positions
Confirmation that all three sides maintain identical proportional ratios
Verification that the triangles share identical area measurements
questions.explanation: Vertical angles provide one pair of congruent angles. To satisfy AA, one more pair of congruent angles is needed. The vertical angles alone are insufficient.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered the AA Similarity Postulate and can identify when two triangles are similar by angle congruence.
scoreMessages.70: Good work! You understand AA but review why only two pairs of angles are needed and how to identify congruent angles in diagrams with parallel lines.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Focus on: AA requires two pairs of congruent angles; the third pair follows automatically; parallel lines and vertical angles are common sources of angle congruence.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! AA Similarity: if $\angle A \cong \angle D$ and $\angle B \cong \angle E$, then $\triangle ABC \sim \triangle DEF$. The third angles match automatically by the Triangle Angle Sum Theorem.
